md: An **arc** is part of a circle's circumference. A **sector** is the pie-slice area cut off by two radii. Both are measured by their **central angle** — the angle at the center of the circle that opens out to the arc or sector.

blocks.title: The big idea — proportional pieces
blocks.body: An arc is just *part* of the full circumference. A sector is just *part* of the full area. The fraction is determined by the central angle: $\dfrac{\theta}{360°}$ of the whole circle.

md: Try the widget — adjust the radius and the sector angle. Watch the formulas compute live.
A circle has radius 6. Find the length of an arc with central angle 60°.
Same circle (r = 6). Find the area of a sector with central angle 90°.
(A 90° sector is exactly a quarter of the circle. Indeed $9\pi$ is one-fourth of $36\pi$.)

blocks.title: Common fractions worth memorizing
blocks.body: $\theta = 90°$ → quarter. $\theta = 180°$ → half (semicircle). $\theta = 60°$ → sixth. $\theta = 45°$ → eighth. $\theta = 30°$ → twelfth. Knowing the fraction shortcuts the arithmetic.

md: ### A connection — arc length in radians
If you measure the angle in *radians* instead of degrees, the arc-length formula simplifies dramatically:
On a unit circle ($r = 1$), the arc length is simply $\theta$ — that's literally the *definition* of a radian. This is why radians are calculus's favorite unit.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Arc and sector are pieces of the whole, scaled by $\theta / 360°$. Arc length: $\frac{\theta}{360} \cdot 2\pi r$. Sector area: $\frac{\theta}{360} \cdot \pi r^2$. With radians, arc length simplifies to $r\theta$.
